food_subgroup: Cooked white rice mixed with yogurt and tempering

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Curd Rice (Thayir Sadam). The nearest parent is a dairy-rice mixed dish. A new concept for South Indian curd rice is recommended for FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice mixed with dairy condiment, South Indian variety rice (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for South Indian curd rice; A03SL (rice-based mixed dish with dairy) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for curd rice or thayir sadam as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of curd rice weighs approximately 250 g, providing around 305 kcal, 8.75 g protein, and 205 mg calcium.

nutrient_source.title: Curd Rice — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry G00010240
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for Curd Rice entry G00010240. All macros and micronutrients are taken as reported. ICMR-NIN is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Mixed Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database: estimated from cooked white rice with full-fat yogurt
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked white rice mixed with full-fat yogurt. The yogurt protein and fat matrix slows starch digestion, reducing the effective GI relative to plain white rice (~72). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 53.75) / 100 ≈ 31.2 for a 250 g bowl. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 168 mg per 100 g (420 mg per 250 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g bowl of curd rice contributes approximately 420 mg sodium — about 18% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — so individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and limit additional salt in the tempering; the calcium content (82 mg/100 g) from yogurt may provide a modest blood-pressure benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~58) and glycemic load of ~31 per 250 g bowl
type2_diabetes.guidance: Curd rice has a medium GI of approximately 58, moderated by the protein and fat in yogurt slowing starch digestion; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to reduce the glycemic load to below 20 per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g), negligible trans fat, and probiotic content from yogurt
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Curd rice is relatively low in saturated fat and contains no trans fat; the probiotic cultures in yogurt may support a favorable lipid profile, and the dish can be included in a heart-healthy diet when prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic content and anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, mustard seeds)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Probiotics in yogurt support gut microbiome health linked to immune modulation; ginger and mustard seeds in the tempering have documented anti-inflammatory properties, making curd rice a suitable dish for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curd Rice (Thayir Sadam / Daddojanam / Mosaranna) is a quintessential South Indian comfort food made by gently mashing warm cooked white rice and mixing it with fresh plain yogurt (curd) until the mixture is creamy, smooth, and mildly sour. The dish is finished with a hot oil tempering (tadka) of mustard seeds, urad dal, dried red chilli, curry leaves, fresh ginger, and green chilli, which adds a fragrant, slightly spicy counterpoint to the cooling yogurt base. It is traditionally served at room temperature or slightly warm, often garnished with pomegranate seeds and fresh coriander. Nutritionally, curd rice provides approximately 122 kcal per 100 g, with 3.5 g protein, 2.8 g fat, and 21.5 g carbohydrate. It is a meaningful source of calcium (82 mg/100 g) and vitamin B12 (0.25 mcg/100 g) from the yogurt, and contains live probiotic cultures that support digestive health. The medium glycemic index (~58) is lower than plain white rice due to the protein and fat matrix of the yogurt slowing starch digestion. Curd rice is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. It is traditionally the final dish in a South Indian banana-leaf meal, served to calm the palate after spicy curries, and is widely consumed across Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, and Karnataka.
common_user_queries: is curd rice good for weight loss
meal_context: last course of a South Indian thali

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (entry G00010240), which is the approved primary regional source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2. All core macros and micronutrients are reported as exact values with confidence 1.0. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous food (cooked white rice with full-fat yogurt) with confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for curd rice is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods entry G00010240. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN source for this preparation). All core macros (protein_g 3.5, fat_g 2.8, carbohydrate_g 21.5) are non-zero and sourced from ICMR-NIN. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database; a direct GI measurement for curd rice is not available in the database as of 2026. The description field in the input JSON references 'Long Grain Basmati Rice from the hills of India, parboiled' which appears to describe the rice component only; the dish as prepared uses cooked white rice mixed with yogurt and tempering as confirmed by ICMR-NIN and culinary sources.
